title: Reranking Capstone
concept: Building a full two-stage retrieve-then-rerank pipeline and measuring its NDCG improvement; 'Next up: RAG Architecture Foundations.'.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we collected 'relevance judgments'. What are these? 'relevance judgments'. What are they?

options.text: The speed in milliseconds it takes to run a database search query.
Labels indicating how relevant a document is to a query, collected from clicks or human ratings.
The raw vector embeddings generated by an encoder model.

exercises.explain: Relevance judgments represent ground-truth labels (like a score from 0 to 3) indicating how well a document answers a specific query.

cards.title: Putting It All Together
cards.body: We have learned how to fetch documents and gather user feedback. Now, let's connect everything together into a high-performance search pipeline and prove it works using real metrics!

cards.title: Two-Stage Pipeline
cards.body: A two-stage pipeline combines a fast vector search (Stage 1) to fetch a candidate pool with a powerful cross-encoder (Stage 2) to re-order them. We measure its success using NDCG, which rewards putting highly relevant documents at the very top.

cards.title: The Talent Show Pipeline
cards.body: Think of your retrieval pipeline like a talent scout. Stage 1 (retriever) scans the globe to quickly find the top 100 singers. Stage 2 (rerank) is a panel of expert judges who spend time listening closely to rank the absolute best 5.

cards.title: Relevance in Action
cards.body: In a movie search engine, Stage 1 quickly grabs 50 movies matching 'space adventure'. Stage 2 recalculates scores, pushing 'Interstellar' above weaker matches, raising the NDCG score from 0.72 to 0.91.

cards.title: The Latency Myth
cards.body: You might think reranking is too slow for production. In reality, because the heavy cross-encoder only processes a small candidate pool (like 50 documents) instead of the entire database, it adds negligible delay while significantly boosting search quality.

cards.title: Pipeline Comparison
cards.body: Comparing a single-stage vector search against a complete two-stage retrieve-and-rerank pipeline.

left.text: Fast but misses deep context; highly relevant docs might get stuck at the bottom of the list.

right.text: Combines fast candidate retrieval with deep cross-encoder sorting to put the best results first.

exercises.prompt: Which metric is best suited to measure the ranking quality improvement after adding a reranker to your search pipeline?
exercises.explain: NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) is the industry standard metric for evaluating ranked search results because it accounts for both relevance and position.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a reranker successfully moves the most relevant document from position 10 to position 1, the NDCG score will increase.
exercises.explain: NDCG increases when highly relevant documents are successfully moved from lower positions to the top spots.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to describe the search architecture.
exercises.sentence: We build a two-stage pipeline to achieve both high retrieval speed and precise ranking.
exercises.explain: A two-stage pipeline balances speed and accuracy by using a fast retriever first, followed by a precise reranker.

exercises.prompt: Identify the output generated by an AI assistant showing a pipeline configuration.

options.text: I built a search pipeline where BM25 fetches the top 100 documents and Cohere Rerank sorts them. It works really well!
We saw our search quality jump significantly once we introduced the cross-encoder step on top of our standard vector database queries.

exercises.explain: Option C is clearly written by an AI because it outputs a generic, structured JSON response showcasing search pipeline code rather than a human-style conversational explanation.

exercises.prompt: You are building a production search engine with millions of documents. What is the optimal architecture to maximize relevance (measured by NDCG) while keeping latency low?

options.text: Rerank the entire database of millions of documents directly using the cross-encoder.
Retrieve the top 100 candidates via vector search, rerank those 100 with a cross-encoder, and evaluate using NDCG.
Replace vector search entirely with keyword matching to bypass the need for any stage-two ranking.
Use an LLM to generate ranking scores for all database items simultaneously.

exercises.explain: A two-stage pipeline keeps search lightning-fast by using vector search on millions of items first, then uses the slower but highly accurate reranker on just the top results to optimize NDCG. 'Next up: RAG Architecture Foundations.'
